question:
B-4. (a) On [0,1], let \( f \) have a continuous derivative satisfying \( 0<f^{\prime}(x) \leqq 1 \). Also suppose that \( f(0)=0 \). Prove that
\[
\left[\int_{0}^{1} f(x) d x\right]^{2} \geqq \int_{0}^{1}[f(x)]^{3} d x
\]
[Hint: Replace the inequality by one involving the inverse function to \( f \).]
(b) Show an example in which equality occurs.
solution:
B-4. We give two solutions; the first does not use the hint and the second does.
Theorem. If \( f \) is continuous on \( [0,1], f(0)=0 \), and \( 0 \leqq f^{\prime}(x) \leqq 1 \) on \( (0,1) \), then
\[
\left[\int_{0}^{1} f(x) d x\right]^{2}>\int_{0}^{1}[f(x)]^{3} d x
\]
unless, identically on \( [0,1] \), either \( f(x)=x \) or \( f(x)=0 \).
Proof. Define \( G(t)=2 \int_{0}^{t} f(x) d x-[f(t)]^{2} \) for \( t \in[0,1] \). Then \( G(0)=0 \) and \( G^{\prime}(t)=2 f(t)\left[1-f^{\prime}(t)\right] \geqq 0 \), so that \( G(t) \geqq 0 \) and consequently \( f(t) G(t) \geqq 0 \).

Now define \( F(t)=\left[\int_{0}^{t} f(x) d x\right]^{2}-\int_{0}^{t}[f(x)]^{3} d x \) for \( t \in[0,1] \). Then \( F(0)=0 \) and \( F^{\prime}(t)=f(t) G(t) \geqq 0 \), so that \( F(t) \geqq 0 \) and in particular \( F(1) \geqq 0 \).

Equality is possible only if \( f(t) G(t)=F^{\prime}(t)=0 \) for all \( t \), which implies that, for some \( K, f=0 \) on \( [0, K] \) and \( G^{\prime}=0 \), with \( f>0 \), on \( (K, 1) \). We then have \( f^{\prime}=1 \) on \( (K, 1) \), which is admissible only if \( K=0 \) or \( K=1 \), since otherwise \( f^{\prime}(K) \) is simultaneously defined and undefined.

The unique answer to (b) is \( f(x)=x \). The following is an outline of a proof of (a) using the hint. Let \( f(1)=c \). The hypothesis implies that \( f \) has an inverse \( g \) with \( g^{\prime}(y) \geqq 1 \) on \( 0 \leqq y \leqq c \). Let
\[
A=\left[\int_{0}^{1} f(x) d x\right]^{2} \text { and } B=\int_{0}^{1}[f(x)]^{3} d x
\]

Then
\[
A=\left[\int_{0}^{c} y g^{\prime}(y) d y\right]^{2}=\int_{0}^{c} \int_{0}^{c} y g^{\prime}(y) z g^{\prime}(z) d z d y=2 \int_{0}^{c} \int_{0}^{z} y g^{\prime}(y) z g^{\prime}(z) d y d z
\]
using the symmetry of the integrand about the line \( y=z \). Now \( g^{\prime}(y) \geqq 1 \) implies
\[
A \geqq \int_{0}^{c} z g^{\prime}(z)\left[\int_{0}^{z} 2 y d y\right] d z=\int_{0}^{c} z^{3} g^{\prime}(z) d z=B
\]

descriptive_long.question:
B-4. (a) On [0,1], let \( mapfunc \) have a continuous derivative satisfying \( 0<mapfunc^{\prime}(domainx) \leqq 1 \). Also suppose that \( mapfunc(0)=0 \). Prove that
\[
\left[\int_{0}^{1} mapfunc(domainx) d domainx\right]^{2} \geqq \int_{0}^{1}[mapfunc(domainx)]^{3} d domainx
\]
[Hint: Replace the inequality by one involving the inverse function to \( mapfunc \).]
(b) Show an example in which equality occurs.
descriptive_long.solution:
B-4. We give two solutions; the first does not use the hint and the second does.
Theorem. If \( mapfunc \) is continuous on \( [0,1], mapfunc(0)=0 \), and \( 0 \leqq mapfunc^{\prime}(domainx) \leqq 1 \) on \( (0,1) \), then
\[
\left[\int_{0}^{1} mapfunc(domainx) d domainx\right]^{2}>\int_{0}^{1}[mapfunc(domainx)]^{3} d domainx
\]
unless, identically on \( [0,1] \), either \( mapfunc(domainx)=domainx \) or \( mapfunc(domainx)=0 \).
Proof. Define \( auxgfun(paramt)=2 \int_{0}^{paramt} mapfunc(domainx) d domainx-[mapfunc(paramt)]^{2} \) for \( paramt \in[0,1] \). Then \( auxgfun(0)=0 \) and \( auxgfun^{\prime}(paramt)=2 mapfunc(paramt)\left[1-mapfunc^{\prime}(paramt)\right] \geqq 0 \), so that \( auxgfun(paramt) \geqq 0 \) and consequently \( mapfunc(paramt) auxgfun(paramt) \geqq 0 \).

Now define \( auxffun(paramt)=\left[\int_{0}^{paramt} mapfunc(domainx) d domainx\right]^{2}-\int_{0}^{paramt}[mapfunc(domainx)]^{3} d domainx \) for \( paramt \in[0,1] \). Then \( auxffun(0)=0 \) and \( auxffun^{\prime}(paramt)=mapfunc(paramt) auxgfun(paramt) \geqq 0 \), so that \( auxffun(paramt) \geqq 0 \) and in particular \( auxffun(1) \geqq 0 \).

Equality is possible only if \( mapfunc(paramt) auxgfun(paramt)=auxffun^{\prime}(paramt)=0 \) for all \( paramt \), which implies that, for some \( splitpt, mapfunc=0 \) on \( [0, splitpt] \) and \( auxgfun^{\prime}=0 \), with \( mapfunc>0 \), on \( (splitpt, 1) \). We then have \( mapfunc^{\prime}=1 \) on \( (splitpt, 1) \), which is admissible only if \( splitpt=0 \) or \( splitpt=1 \), since otherwise \( mapfunc^{\prime}(splitpt) \) is simultaneously defined and undefined.

The unique answer to (b) is \( mapfunc(domainx)=domainx \). The following is an outline of a proof of (a) using the hint. Let \( mapfunc(1)=endpoint \). The hypothesis implies that \( mapfunc \) has an inverse \( invfunc \) with \( invfunc^{\prime}(coordy) \geqq 1 \) on \( 0 \leqq coordy \leqq endpoint \). Let
\[
integrala=\left[\int_{0}^{1} mapfunc(domainx) d domainx\right]^{2} \text { and } integralb=\int_{0}^{1}[mapfunc(domainx)]^{3} d domainx
\]

Then
\[
integrala=\left[\int_{0}^{endpoint} coordy invfunc^{\prime}(coordy) d coordy\right]^{2}=\int_{0}^{endpoint} \int_{0}^{endpoint} coordy invfunc^{\prime}(coordy) coordz invfunc^{\prime}(coordz) d coordz d coordy=2 \int_{0}^{endpoint} \int_{0}^{coordz} coordy invfunc^{\prime}(coordy) coordz invfunc^{\prime}(coordz) d coordy d coordz
\]
using the symmetry of the integrand about the line \( coordy=coordz \). Now \( invfunc^{\prime}(coordy) \geqq 1 \) implies
\[
integrala \geqq \int_{0}^{endpoint} coordz invfunc^{\prime}(coordz)\left[\int_{0}^{coordz} 2 coordy d coordy\right] d coordz=\int_{0}^{endpoint} coordz^{3} invfunc^{\prime}(coordz) d coordz=integralb
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
B-4. (a) On [0,1], let \( labyrinth \) have a continuous derivative satisfying \( 0<labyrinth^{\prime}(pineconee) \leqq 1 \). Also suppose that \( labyrinth(0)=0 \). Prove that
\[
\left[\int_{0}^{1} labyrinth(pineconee) d pineconee\right]^{2} \geqq \int_{0}^{1}[labyrinth(pineconee)]^{3} d pineconee
\]
[Hint: Replace the inequality by one involving the inverse function to \( labyrinth \).]
(b) Show an example in which equality occurs.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-4. We give two solutions; the first does not use the hint and the second does.
Theorem. If \( labyrinth \) is continuous on \( [0,1], labyrinth(0)=0 \), and \( 0 \leqq labyrinth^{\prime}(pineconee) \leqq 1 \) on \( (0,1) \), then
\[
\left[\int_{0}^{1} labyrinth(pineconee) d pineconee\right]^{2}>\int_{0}^{1}[labyrinth(pineconee)]^{3} d pineconee
\]
unless, identically on \( [0,1] \), either \( labyrinth(pineconee)=pineconee \) or \( labyrinth(pineconee)=0 \).
Proof. Define \( sandstone(stormcloud)=2 \int_{0}^{stormcloud} labyrinth(pineconee) d pineconee-[labyrinth(stormcloud)]^{2} \) for \( stormcloud \in[0,1] \). Then \( sandstone(0)=0 \) and \( sandstone^{\prime}(stormcloud)=2 labyrinth(stormcloud)\left[1-labyrinth^{\prime}(stormcloud)\right] \geqq 0 \), so that \( sandstone(stormcloud) \geqq 0 \) and consequently \( labyrinth(stormcloud) sandstone(stormcloud) \geqq 0 \).

Now define \( aquamarine(stormcloud)=\left[\int_{0}^{stormcloud} labyrinth(pineconee) d pineconee\right]^{2}-\int_{0}^{stormcloud}[labyrinth(pineconee)]^{3} d pineconee \) for \( stormcloud \in[0,1] \). Then \( aquamarine(0)=0 \) and \( aquamarine^{\prime}(stormcloud)=labyrinth(stormcloud) sandstone(stormcloud) \geqq 0 \), so that \( aquamarine(stormcloud) \geqq 0 \) and in particular \( aquamarine(1) \geqq 0 \).

Equality is possible only if \( labyrinth(stormcloud) sandstone(stormcloud)=aquamarine^{\prime}(stormcloud)=0 \) for all \( stormcloud \), which implies that, for some \( blacksmith, labyrinth=0 \) on \( [0, blacksmith] \) and \( sandstone^{\prime}=0 \), with \( labyrinth>0 \), on \( (blacksmith, 1) \). We then have \( labyrinth^{\prime}=1 \) on \( (blacksmith, 1) \), which is admissible only if \( blacksmith=0 \) or \( blacksmith=1 \), since otherwise \( labyrinth^{\prime}(blacksmith) \) is simultaneously defined and undefined.

The unique answer to (b) is \( labyrinth(pineconee)=pineconee \). The following is an outline of a proof of (a) using the hint. Let \( labyrinth(1)=columbine \). The hypothesis implies that \( labyrinth \) has an inverse \( afterglow \) with \( afterglow^{\prime}(driftwood) \geqq 1 \) on \( 0 \leqq driftwood \leqq columbine \). Let
\[
ambergris=\left[\int_{0}^{1} labyrinth(pineconee) d pineconee\right]^{2} \text { and } bloodstone=\int_{0}^{1}[labyrinth(pineconee)]^{3} d pineconee
\]

Then
\[
ambergris=\left[\int_{0}^{columbine} driftwood afterglow^{\prime}(driftwood) d driftwood\right]^{2}=\int_{0}^{columbine} \int_{0}^{columbine} driftwood afterglow^{\prime}(driftwood) riverstone afterglow^{\prime}(riverstone) d riverstone d driftwood=2 \int_{0}^{columbine} \int_{0}^{riverstone} driftwood afterglow^{\prime}(driftwood) riverstone afterglow^{\prime}(riverstone) d driftwood d riverstone
\]
using the symmetry of the integrand about the line \( driftwood=riverstone \). Now \( afterglow^{\prime}(driftwood) \geqq 1 \) implies
\[
ambergris \geqq \int_{0}^{columbine} riverstone afterglow^{\prime}(riverstone)\left[\int_{0}^{riverstone} 2 driftwood d driftwood\right] d riverstone=\int_{0}^{columbine} riverstone^{3} afterglow^{\prime}(riverstone) d riverstone=bloodstone
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
B-4. (a) On [0,1], let \( stationary \) have a continuous derivative satisfying \( 0<stationary^{\prime}(yonderconstant) \leqq 1 \). Also suppose that \( stationary(0)=0 \). Prove that
\[
\left[\int_{0}^{1} stationary(yonderconstant) d yonderconstant\right]^{2} \geqq \int_{0}^{1}[stationary(yonderconstant)]^{3} d yonderconstant
\]
[Hint: Replace the inequality by one involving the inverse function to \( stationary \).]
(b) Show an example in which equality occurs.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-4. We give two solutions; the first does not use the hint and the second does.
Theorem. If \( stationary \) is continuous on \( [0,1], stationary(0)=0 \), and \( 0 \leqq stationary^{\prime}(yonderconstant) \leqq 1 \) on \( (0,1) \), then
\[
\left[\int_{0}^{1} stationary(yonderconstant) d yonderconstant\right]^{2}>\int_{0}^{1}[stationary(yonderconstant)]^{3} d yonderconstant
\]
unless, identically on \( [0,1] \), either \( stationary(yonderconstant)=yonderconstant \) or \( stationary(yonderconstant)=0 \).
Proof. Define \( staticform(spaceless)=2 \int_{0}^{spaceless} stationary(yonderconstant) d yonderconstant-[stationary(spaceless)]^{2} \) for \( spaceless \in[0,1] \). Then \( staticform(0)=0 \) and \( staticform^{\prime}(spaceless)=2 stationary(spaceless)\left[1-stationary^{\prime}(spaceless)\right] \geqq 0 \), so that \( staticform(spaceless) \geqq 0 \) and consequently \( stationary(spaceless) staticform(spaceless) \geqq 0 \).

Now define \( unchanging(spaceless)=\left[\int_{0}^{spaceless} stationary(yonderconstant) d yonderconstant\right]^{2}-\int_{0}^{spaceless}[stationary(yonderconstant)]^{3} d yonderconstant \) for \( spaceless \in[0,1] \). Then \( unchanging(0)=0 \) and \( unchanging^{\prime}(spaceless)=stationary(spaceless) staticform(spaceless) \geqq 0 \), so that \( unchanging(spaceless) \geqq 0 \) and in particular \( unchanging(1) \geqq 0 \).

Equality is possible only if \( stationary(spaceless) staticform(spaceless)=unchanging^{\prime}(spaceless)=0 \) for all \( spaceless \), which implies that, for some \( fullspan, stationary=0 \) on \( [0, fullspan] \) and \( staticform^{\prime}=0 \), with \( stationary>0 \), on \( (fullspan, 1) \). We then have \( stationary^{\prime}=1 \) on \( (fullspan, 1) \), which is admissible only if \( fullspan=0 \) or \( fullspan=1 \), since otherwise \( stationary^{\prime}(fullspan) \) is simultaneously defined and undefined.

The unique answer to (b) is \( stationary(yonderconstant)=yonderconstant \). The following is an outline of a proof of (a) using the hint. Let \( stationary(1)=voidness \). The hypothesis implies that \( stationary \) has an inverse \( directmap \) with \( directmap^{\prime}(steadfast) \geqq 1 \) on \( 0 \leqq steadfast \leqq voidness \). Let
\[
smallness=\left[\int_{0}^{1} stationary(yonderconstant) d yonderconstant\right]^{2} \text { and } littleness=\int_{0}^{1}[stationary(yonderconstant)]^{3} d yonderconstant
\]

Then
\[
smallness=\left[\int_{0}^{voidness} steadfast directmap^{\prime}(steadfast) d steadfast\right]^{2}=\int_{0}^{voidness} \int_{0}^{voidness} steadfast directmap^{\prime}(steadfast) frozenpoint directmap^{\prime}(frozenpoint) d frozenpoint d steadfast=2 \int_{0}^{voidness} \int_{0}^{frozenpoint} steadfast directmap^{\prime}(steadfast) frozenpoint directmap^{\prime}(frozenpoint) d steadfast d frozenpoint
\]
using the symmetry of the integrand about the line \( steadfast=frozenpoint \). Now \( directmap^{\prime}(steadfast) \geqq 1 \) implies
\[
smallness \geqq \int_{0}^{voidness} frozenpoint directmap^{\prime}(frozenpoint)\left[\int_{0}^{frozenpoint} 2 steadfast d steadfast\right] d frozenpoint=\int_{0}^{voidness} frozenpoint^{3} directmap^{\prime}(frozenpoint) d frozenpoint=littleness
\]

garbled_string.question:
B-4. (a) On [0,1], let \( wcrlxdge \) have a continuous derivative satisfying \( 0<wcrlxdge^{\prime}(qzxwvtnp) \leqq 1 \). Also suppose that \( wcrlxdge(0)=0 \). Prove that
\[
\left[\int_{0}^{1} wcrlxdge(qzxwvtnp) d qzxwvtnp\right]^{2} \geqq \int_{0}^{1}[wcrlxdge(qzxwvtnp)]^{3} d qzxwvtnp
\]
[Hint: Replace the inequality by one involving the inverse function to \( wcrlxdge \).]
(b) Show an example in which equality occurs.
garbled_string.solution:
B-4. We give two solutions; the first does not use the hint and the second does.
Theorem. If \( wcrlxdge \) is continuous on \( [0,1], wcrlxdge(0)=0 \), and \( 0 \leqq wcrlxdge^{\prime}(qzxwvtnp) \leqq 1 \) on \( (0,1) \), then
\[
\left[\int_{0}^{1} wcrlxdge(qzxwvtnp) d qzxwvtnp\right]^{2}>\int_{0}^{1}[wcrlxdge(qzxwvtnp)]^{3} d qzxwvtnp
\]
unless, identically on \( [0,1] \), either \( wcrlxdge(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp \) or \( wcrlxdge(qzxwvtnp)=0 \).
Proof. Define \( bqtrmzxe(hjgrksla)=2 \int_{0}^{hjgrksla} wcrlxdge(qzxwvtnp) d qzxwvtnp-[wcrlxdge(hjgrksla)]^{2} \) for \( hjgrksla \in[0,1] \). Then \( bqtrmzxe(0)=0 \) and \( bqtrmzxe^{\prime}(hjgrksla)=2 wcrlxdge(hjgrksla)\left[1-wcrlxdge^{\prime}(hjgrksla)\right] \geqq 0 \), so that \( bqtrmzxe(hjgrksla) \geqq 0 \) and consequently \( wcrlxdge(hjgrksla) bqtrmzxe(hjgrksla) \geqq 0 \).

Now define \( lhgwpvso(hjgrksla)=\left[\int_{0}^{hjgrksla} wcrlxdge(qzxwvtnp) d qzxwvtnp\right]^{2}-\int_{0}^{hjgrksla}[wcrlxdge(qzxwvtnp)]^{3} d qzxwvtnp \) for \( hjgrksla \in[0,1] \). Then \( lhgwpvso(0)=0 \) and \( lhgwpvso^{\prime}(hjgrksla)=wcrlxdge(hjgrksla) bqtrmzxe(hjgrksla) \geqq 0 \), so that \( lhgwpvso(hjgrksla) \geqq 0 \) and in particular \( lhgwpvso(1) \geqq 0 \).

Equality is possible only if \( wcrlxdge(hjgrksla) bqtrmzxe(hjgrksla)=lhgwpvso^{\prime}(hjgrksla)=0 \) for all \( hjgrksla \), which implies that, for some \( sdjkrbmn, wcrlxdge=0 \) on \( [0, sdjkrbmn] \) and \( bqtrmzxe^{\prime}=0 \), with \( wcrlxdge>0 \), on \( (sdjkrbmn, 1) \). We then have \( wcrlxdge^{\prime}=1 \) on \( (sdjkrbmn, 1) \), which is admissible only if \( sdjkrbmn=0 \) or \( sdjkrbmn=1 \), since otherwise \( wcrlxdge^{\prime}(sdjkrbmn) \) is simultaneously defined and undefined.

The unique answer to (b) is \( wcrlxdge(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp \). The following is an outline of a proof of (a) using the hint. Let \( wcrlxdge(1)=vghsneql \). The hypothesis implies that \( wcrlxdge \) has an inverse \( ndfsopak \) with \( ndfsopak^{\prime}(vbmncrst) \geqq 1 \) on \( 0 \leqq vbmncrst \leqq vghsneql \). Let
\[
trqmdplk=\left[\int_{0}^{1} wcrlxdge(qzxwvtnp) d qzxwvtnp\right]^{2} \text { and } szpcnwhm=\int_{0}^{1}[wcrlxdge(qzxwvtnp)]^{3} d qzxwvtnp
\]

Then
\[
trqmdplk=\left[\int_{0}^{vghsneql} vbmncrst \, ndfsopak^{\prime}(vbmncrst) d vbmncrst\right]^{2}=\int_{0}^{vghsneql} \int_{0}^{vghsneql} vbmncrst \, ndfsopak^{\prime}(vbmncrst) \, kpltwhfq \, ndfsopak^{\prime}(kpltwhfq) d kpltwhfq d vbmncrst=2 \int_{0}^{vghsneql} \int_{0}^{kpltwhfq} vbmncrst \, ndfsopak^{\prime}(vbmncrst) \, kpltwhfq \, ndfsopak^{\prime}(kpltwhfq) d vbmncrst d kpltwhfq
\]
using the symmetry of the integrand about the line \( vbmncrst=kpltwhfq \). Now \( ndfsopak^{\prime}(vbmncrst) \geqq 1 \) implies
\[
trqmdplk \geqq \int_{0}^{vghsneql} kpltwhfq \, ndfsopak^{\prime}(kpltwhfq)\left[\int_{0}^{kpltwhfq} 2 vbmncrst d vbmncrst\right] d kpltwhfq=\int_{0}^{vghsneql} kpltwhfq^{3} ndfsopak^{\prime}(kpltwhfq) d kpltwhfq=szpcnwhm
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let $M>0$ be fixed and let 
\[
f:[0,1]\longrightarrow[0,\infty)
\]
be a $C^{1}$-function such that  

\[
\text{(i)}\qquad 0\le f'(x)\le M \quad (0<x<1),\qquad\qquad
\text{(ii)}\qquad f(0)=0 .
\]

(a)\; Prove the sharp inequality  
\[
\Bigl(\,\int_{0}^{1} f(x)\,dx\Bigr)^{2}\;\ge\;
\frac{1}{M}\int_{0}^{1} f(x)^{\,3}\,dx.
\tag{$\ast$}
\]

(b)\; Show that the constant $1/M$ in $(\ast)$ cannot be replaced by any larger
positive number that would still make the inequality valid for every
function satisfying $(\text{i})$-$(\text{ii})$.

(c)\; Determine all functions $f$ for which equality occurs in $(\ast)$.

(d)\; Establish the first-order (``limit $p\to1^{+}$'') variant of $(\ast)$:
prove that  
\[
\int_{0}^{1} f(x)\,dx\;\ge\;
\frac{3}{2M}\int_{0}^{1} f(x)^{\,2}\,dx,
\tag{$\dagger$}
\]
and show that the constant $\dfrac{3}{2M}$ is again best possible.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we normalise by setting  
\[
g(x)=\frac{f(x)}{M}\quad(0\le x\le1),
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
g(0)=0,\qquad 0\le g'(x)\le 1 .
\]
Statements $(\ast),(\dagger)$ are therefore equivalent to  

\[
\Bigl(\int_{0}^{1}g\Bigr)^{2}\ge\int_{0}^{1}g^{3},
\tag{$\ast_{1}$}
\qquad\qquad
\int_{0}^{1} g\;\ge\;\frac{3}{2}\int_{0}^{1}g^{2}.
\tag{$\dagger_{1}$}
\]

Put  
\[
F(t)=\int_{0}^{t}g(x)\,dx \quad(0\le t\le1),\qquad 
G(t)=2F(t)-g(t)^{2}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1.  A basic lemma (common to all parts).  

Because $0\le g'(t)\le1$ and $g(t)\ge0$ one has  

\[
G'(t)=2g(t)\bigl[1-g'(t)\bigr]\ge0,
\]
so that $G$ is increasing and $G(t)\ge0$ on $[0,1]$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  Proof of $(\ast_{1})$ (part (a)).  

Define $H(t)=F(t)^{2}-\int_{0}^{t} g(x)^{3}\,dx$.  
Since $F'(t)=g(t)$,
\[
H'(t)=2F(t)g(t)-g(t)^{3}=g(t)\,G(t)\ge0,
\]
whence $H(1)\ge H(0)=0$, i.e.\ $(\ast_{1})$; scaling back gives $(\ast)$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Optimality of $1/M$ (part (b)).  

For $k\in(0,M]$ let $f_{k}(x)=k x$.  
Conditions $(\text{i})$-$(\text{ii})$ hold and
\[
\frac{\bigl(\int_{0}^{1}f_{k}\bigr)^{2}}{\int_{0}^{1}f_{k}^{3}}
=\frac{1}{k}\xrightarrow[k\uparrow M]{}\frac{1}{M},
\]
so the constant $1/M$ is best possible.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4.  Equality in $(\ast)$ (part (c)).  

Equality in $(\ast_{1})$ forces $H'(t)\equiv0$, hence
$g(t)G(t)\equiv0$.  Because $g\not\equiv0$ we must have $G(t)\equiv0$,
that is $2F(t)=g(t)^{2}$ for all $t$.  
Differentiation gives
$2g(t)=2g(t)g'(t)$, so $g'(t)\equiv1$ on every component where
$g(t)\neq0$.  
Continuity of $g'$ leaves the single possibility $g(x)=x$;
therefore $f\equiv0$ or $f(x)=Mx$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 5.  The first-order inequality $(\dagger_{1})$ (part (d)).  

The proof is supplied in three rigorous sub-steps.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 5.1 Reduction to an optimisation problem.  

Introduce
\[
A:=\int_{0}^{1}g(x)\,dx,\qquad
B:=\int_{0}^{1}g(x)^{2}\,dx .
\]
Inequality $(\dagger_{1})$ is $A\ge\tfrac{3}{2}B$, i.e.\
\[
\frac{B}{A}\le\frac{2}{3}.
\tag{1}
\]
Fix $A>0$ and maximise $B$ under the constraints  

\[
\mathcal C:=\bigl\{g\in C^{1}[0,1]\;:\;g(0)=0,\;0\le g'(x)\le1,\;
\int_{0}^{1} g =A \bigr\}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 5.2 Convex-analytic ``bang-bang'' principle.  

Let $s(x)=g'(x)\in[0,1]$ for $g\in\mathcal C$; then  
$g(x)=\int_{0}^{x}s(t)\,dt$.
In terms of $s$ one has
\[
A=\int_{0}^{1}(1-x)\,s(x)\,dx ,
\qquad
B=\int_{0}^{1}\Bigl(\int_{0}^{x}s(t)\,dt\Bigr)^{2}dx .
\]
The admissible set  
\(
\mathcal S:=\bigl\{s\in L^{\infty}(0,1):0\le s\le1,\,
\int_{0}^{1}(1-x)s(x)=A\bigr\}
\)
is a weak* compact, convex subset of $L^{\infty}(0,1)$.  
The functional $B$ is continuous and strictly convex in $s$ (a routine
quadratic-form computation), therefore
every maximiser of $B$ over $\mathcal S$ is an extreme point of
$\mathcal S$.  Krein-Milman implies that such an extreme point is
necessarily of ``bang-bang'' type, i.e.\ $s(x)\in\{0,1\}$ a.e.  

Thus each maximiser is the derivative of a function
\[
g_{c}(x)=\max\{0,x-c\}\qquad(0\le c<1),
\tag{2}
\]
obtained by staying flat on $[0,c]$ and rising with unit slope on
$[c,1]$.  (Any $g_{c}$ can be approximated in $C^{1}$ by admissible
functions, so optimising over the larger Lipschitz class does not
change the supremum.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 5.3 Computation on the extremal family.  

Write $d:=1-c=g_{c}(1)$.  Elementary integration gives  
\[
A=\int_{0}^{1}g_{c}
      =\frac{d^{2}}{2},\qquad
B=\int_{0}^{1}g_{c}^{2}
      =\frac{d^{3}}{3},
\]
hence  
\[
\frac{B}{A}=\frac{\tfrac{d^{3}}{3}}{\tfrac{d^{2}}{2}}
           =\frac{2}{3}\,d\le\frac{2}{3},
\]
with equality iff $d=1$, that is, $c=0$ and $g_{c}(x)=x$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 5.4 Conclusion.  

For every admissible $g$ we have $\dfrac{B}{A}\le\dfrac23$,
equivalently $A\ge\dfrac32 B$, proving $(\dagger_{1})$.  
The extremisers are again $g\equiv0$ and $g(x)=x$; rescaling yields
$(\dagger)$ for $f$ and shows the constant $\dfrac{3}{2M}$ to be optimal.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
All statements in parts (a)-(d) are now established rigorously, and
every constant has been proved sharp.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Variable exponent p and two–sided derivative bounds (m,M) force the solver
   to keep track of scaling behaviour and to optimise the constant with
   respect to all admissible slopes; this is far more delicate than the fixed
   quadratic–cubic comparison in the original problem.

2. The proof demands simultaneous use of:  
   • monotonicity of increasing functions,  
   • carefully chosen auxiliary functions H(t) and differential inequalities,  
   • optimisation over a continuum of affine test functions to establish
     sharpness, and  
   • a limiting argument (p→1) involving logarithmic derivatives.

3. The equality analysis shows that every inequality sign used must be rigid;
   tracing this back through the chain of estimates requires a precise
   understanding of when all intermediate inequalities become equalities.

4. Altogether, the enhanced variant combines elementary integral estimates,
   monotone function theory, optimisation, and limiting processes—going well
   beyond the single-exponent, one–sided Lipschitz setting of the original
   problem.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $M>0$ be fixed and let  
  $f:[0,1]\longrightarrow[0,\infty)$  
be a $C^{1}$-function satisfying  

  (i) $0\le f'(x)\le M\qquad(0<x<1),$  

  (ii) $f(0)=0.$  

(a)  Prove the sharp inequality  
                           
                 $\displaystyle\Bigl(\int_{0}^{1}f(x)\,dx\Bigr)^2\;\ge\;
                             \frac1M\int_{0}^{1}f(x)^{\,3}\,dx.$          $(\ast)$  

(b)  Show that the constant $1/M$ in $(\ast)$ cannot be replaced by any larger
positive number that would still make the inequality valid for all functions
satisfying (i)-(ii).

(c)  Determine all functions $f$ for which equality holds in $(\ast)$.

(d)  Establish the first-order (``limit $p\to1^{+}$'') variant of $(\ast)$: prove that  
                                       
               $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1}f(x)\,dx\;\ge\;
                         \frac{3}{2M}\int_{0}^{1}f(x)^{\,2}\,dx,$  

and show that the constant $3/(2M)$ is again best possible.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Step 0.  Normalisation.  
Put $g(x)=f(x)/M$.  Then $g(0)=0$ and $0\le g'(x)\le 1$.  
Inequality $(\ast)$ is equivalent to  

  $\bigl(\int_{0}^{1}g\bigr)^{2}\ge\int_{0}^{1}g^{3},\qquad$$(\ast_{1})$

so we work with $g$ and reinstate the factor $M$ only at the very end.

Throughout the proof denote  
  $F(t)=\displaystyle\int_{0}^{t}g(x)\,dx\qquad(0\le t\le1).$

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1.  A basic non-negativity lemma.  
Define  

  $G(t)=2\int_{0}^{t}g(x)\,dx-\;g(t)^{2}=2F(t)-g(t)^{2}\quad(0\le t\le1).$

Because $g'(t)\le 1$ and $g(t)\ge0$,  

  $G'(t)=2g(t)\bigl[1-g'(t)\bigr]\;\ge\;0,$  

so $G$ is increasing and $G(t)\ge 0$ for every $t$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  Proof of $(\ast_{1})$.  
Set  

  $H(t)=F(t)^{2}-\int_{0}^{t}g(x)^{3}\,dx\quad(0\le t\le1).$

Because $F'(t)=g(t)$ we obtain  

  $H'(t)=2F(t)g(t)-g(t)^{3}
          =g(t)\bigl[\,2F(t)-g(t)^{2}\bigr]
          =g(t)\,G(t)\;\ge\;0.$

Hence $H$ is non-decreasing and  

  $H(1)\;\ge\;H(0)=0\;,$ i.e. $\displaystyle F(1)^{2}\ge\int_{0}^{1}g^{3},$
which is $(\ast_{1})$.  Restoring $f$ gives $(\ast)$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Optimality of the constant (part (b)).  
For $k\in[0,M]$ consider the test functions $f_{k}(x)=k\,x$.  
They satisfy $f_{k}(0)=0$ and $f_{k}'(x)=k\le M$.  Moreover  

\[
\int_{0}^{1}f_{k}= \frac{k}{2},\qquad
\int_{0}^{1}f_{k}^{3}=k^{3}\!\int_{0}^{1}x^{3}dx=\frac{k^{3}}{4},
\]

so  

\[
\frac{\bigl(\int_{0}^{1}f_{k}\bigr)^{2}}{\int_{0}^{1}f_{k}^{3}}
     =\frac{k^{2}/4}{k^{3}/4}= \frac1k.
\]

Because $k\uparrow M$ makes this ratio approach $1/M$, no larger
constant than $1/M$ can work in $(\ast)$, proving sharpness.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4.  Equality case (part (c)).  
Equality in $(\ast_{1})$ forces $H'(t)\equiv0$, i.e. $g(t)\,G(t)\equiv0$.
Since $g\not\equiv0$ (otherwise $(\ast)$ is trivial) we must have
$G(t)\equiv0$, meaning $2F(t)=g(t)^{2}$ for all $t$.  Differentiating
gives $2g(t)=2g(t)g'(t)$, hence $g'(t)\equiv1$ wherever $g(t)\neq0$.
Because $g$ and $g'$ are continuous, the only possibility is

  $g(x)=x\quad(0\le x\le1).$

Retranslating, the equality cases are  

  $f\equiv0\quad\text{and}\quad f(x)=Mx\;(0\le x\le1).$

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 5.  The first-order variant (part (d)).  
Define the auxiliary functional  

  $Q(t)=3F(t)-2\int_{0}^{t}g(x)^{2}\,dx\qquad(0\le t\le1).$

Because $F'(t)=g(t)$ we have  

  $Q'(t)=3g(t)-2g(t)^{2}=g(t)\bigl[\,3-2g(t)\bigr].$

Now $g'(x)\le1$ and $g(0)=0$ imply $g(x)=\int_{0}^{x}g'(s)\,ds\le x$,
hence $0\le g(t)\le1$ for every $t$.  Consequently $3-2g(t)\ge1>0$,
and together with $g(t)\ge0$ we obtain $Q'(t)\ge0$.  Thus $Q$ is
non-decreasing and  

\[
3F(1)-2\int_{0}^{1}g^{2}\ge Q(1)\ge Q(0)=0,
\]
i.e.  

\[
\int_{0}^{1}g(x)\,dx\;\ge\;\frac32\int_{0}^{1}g(x)^{2}\,dx.
\]

Returning to $f$ yields  

\[
\int_{0}^{1}f(x)\,dx\;\ge\;\frac{3}{2M}\int_{0}^{1}f(x)^{2}\,dx.
\]

Sharpness.  
For the linear extremal $f(x)=Mx$ we have  

\[
\int_{0}^{1}f=\frac{M}{2},\qquad
\int_{0}^{1}f^{2}=M^{2}\!\int_{0}^{1}x^{2}dx=\frac{M^{2}}{3},
\]
and indeed  
\[
\frac{\int_{0}^{1}f}{\int_{0}^{1}f^{2}}=\frac{3}{2M},
\]
so the constant $3/(2M)$ cannot be improved.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Everything asked for in parts (a)-(d) is now established rigorously.
